data.title: TXNM Energy: Profit From Positive Demographics And Data Center Expansion
data.description:
TXNM Energy, Inc. (NYSE:TXNM) is a regulated electric utility that serves approximately 825,000 customers located throughout Texas and New Mexico:

The company that is currently known as TXNM Energy was formerly known as PNM Resources. Long-time readers might remember that we have discussed PNM Resources in various previous articles a few years ago. At the time, the management of PNM Resources was pushing hard for a merger with Iberdrola’s (OTCPK:IBDSF) Avangrid, which operates in the Mid-Atlantic and New England regions, but regulators were largely opposed to that deal. After much deliberation and back and forth with regulators, the deal was scuttled early last year. As such, it appears that a merger is no longer a possibility for this company, and investors should simply evaluate it as a standalone electric utility operating in New Mexico and Texas.

We can see, however, that the utility operation in New Mexico is vastly larger than the one in Texas. While TXNM does have a presence in some of the most populated areas of Texas (notably the suburbs of Dallas and Houston), it only has approximately 275,000 customers in that state. The company’s customer base in New Mexico is roughly twice the size at 550,000 customers. This comes from the company’s dominance in three of the largest cities in New Mexico – Santa Fe, Rio Rancho, and Albuquerque. As such, New Mexico is somewhat more important for the future of the company, but both states offer growth opportunities for the company and indeed, TXNM Energy appears to be positioned as one of the most rapidly growing utilities that we have discussed in this column. As such, investors interested in both safety and growth may find a lot to like here.

Recent Performance Analysis

We last discussed TXNM Energy on June 14, 2023, when it was still known as PNM Resources. The utility sector (IDU) since that time has been rather volatile due mostly to changing expectations regarding interest rates. Utilities were one of the worst-performing sectors in 2023, and they substantially underperformed the market (SP500):

This disappointing performance reversed itself in 2024, as easing monetary policies resulted in a rebound in these traditionally high-yielding companies. The utility sector gained an impressive 19.07% in 2024, which underperformed the market, but was still overall a better showing than what these companies delivered in the previous year:

As such, we might expect TXNM Energy to have delivered a somewhat mixed performance since our last discussion. The fact that it was forced to abandon the merger with Avangrid could be another blow to its stock performance over the intervening period, but the abandonment of the merger was really no surprise to most investors who followed the company, so it was likely already priced into the stock. This is even more evident when we consider that the company’s stock price did not decline significantly following the announcement that the merger had been abandoned.

Overall, TXNM is up a meager 8.14% since our last discussion, significantly worse than the 20.48% gain that the S&P 500 Index delivered over the same period:

The company’s stock also underperformed if we look at it on a total return basis:

We can clearly see underperformance against both the S&P 500 Total Return Index (SP500TR) and the U.S. Utilities Index, so there is certainly nothing impressive here. The fact that the company’s stock has underperformed may represent a buying opportunity, though, so we should investigate further.

About TXNM Energy

As stated in the introduction, TXNM Energy is a regulated electric and natural gas company that serves approximately 825,000 customers in the states of Texas and New Mexico. Of the two, New Mexico is the more important state, as it is home to about two-thirds of the company’s customers.

The common perception of New Mexico in particular is that the state is a vast desert with a very low population. There is some truth to this, as New Mexico is one of the least densely populated states in the United States. The U.S. Census Bureau estimates that the state currently has a population of 2,130,256 people spread out over 121,590 square miles. This would give the state an estimated population density of 17.52 people per square mile, lower than all except for five states (South Dakota, North Dakota, Montana, Wyoming, and Alaska). However, a simple look at the population density does not tell the whole story, since the vast majority of the population of New Mexico lives in one of a handful of large cities. According to the World Population Review, approximately 557,198 people, or roughly one-fourth of the state’s population, live in Albuquerque. TXNM Energy is the primary provider of electricity to Albuquerque, so this alone gives the company a dominant presence in the state. In addition, another estimated 90,125 call the city of Santa Fe home. These two cities thus have a combined estimated population of 647,323 people, which is almost one-third of the entire state’s population.

As we can see in the map above, TXNM Energy serves both of these cities and the area between them. The area between them includes the city of Rio Rancho, which has a current population of 114,800 people. If we add this number to the previous figure, we get a population of 762,123 people, which is 35.78% of the state’s estimated population. This basically results in the company having a much larger customer base than we might expect from a utility that is operating in New Mexico.

TXNM Energy is thus a larger utility than might be expected. At the current stock price, the company boasts a market capitalization of $4.64 billion and an enterprise value of $10.52 billion. Here is how that compares to a few other comparable utilities:

Company Name

Market Capitalization

Enterprise Value

TXNM Energy

$4.64 billion

$10.52 billion

NorthWestern Energy Group (NWE)

$3.41 billion

$6.51 billion

Avista Corporation (AVA)

$3.22 billion

$6.34 billion

Otter Tail Corporation (OTTR)

$3.25 billion

$3.99 billion

Black Hills Corporation (BKH)

$4.20 billion

$8.65 billion

For the purposes of constructing this chart, I tried to include only those utility companies that primarily serve some of the most rural and sparsely populated regions of the United States. Out of all of them, we can see that TXNM Energy is the largest, which is likely due to its service territory including three cities that contain a significant portion of the New Mexican population.

Fortunately, TXNM Energy is one utility that appears to have demographics working in its favor. World Population Review projects that the population of Albuquerque will decline going forward:

As of 2025, Albuquerque is estimated to have a population of 557,200 people, down from its 2020 peak population of 564,900 people. The most recent estimate puts the city’s population as declining at a 0.28% rate, which would, if it continues, take the city’s population down to 551,000 in 2029. That represents a cumulative population decline of 6,200 people through the end of this decade.

The population of Santa Fe is growing, but it is unlikely that it will be able to offset the population decline in Albuquerque. World Population Review puts the city’s population at approximately 90,100 today (estimated) and predicts that it will grow at a 0.53% rate through 2029:

This rate of change puts the city’s population at approximately 92,000 in 2029, which is only an increase of 900 people over today’s level. That is obviously not enough to offset the estimated population decline of 6,200 people in Albuquerque.

There is one final city in the company’s service territory that may change this situation for the better. This is Rio Rancho, which is directly north of Albuquerque. I am not sure why the company did not include this city on the service territory map that it provided in its most recent investor presentation, given that Rio Rancho is actually larger than Santa Fe. Perhaps many people would consider it to be part of the greater Albuquerque area and not worth mentioning separately. For our analysis today, though, it is certainly worth mentioning given that this city’s projected population growth will offset much of the decline that we see in Albuquerque. According to the World Population Review, Rio Rancho currently has a population of 114,800 people, which will grow at a 1.84% rate through 2029:

If this projection proves to be accurate, then Rio Rancho’s population will increase by approximately 8,300 over the next five years. That completely offsets the projected population decline seen in Albuquerque. Furthermore, when we combine the population growth in Rio Rancho with that of Santa Fe, we actually get a growing population across the three largest New Mexican cities in the company’s service territory. Thus, the demographics are in the company’s favor here, and it should experience some tailwinds just from the growing population within its service territory.

Of course, TXNM Energy will need to invest in infrastructure expansion in order to actually benefit from and provide electric utility services to a growing population. TXNM Energy is planning to do exactly this. TXNM Energy recently presented its five-year capital plan to its investors, which calls for it to invest approximately $7.8 billion into its utility-grade infrastructure over the 2025 to 2029 period:

Perhaps interestingly, the majority of this spending is directed towards the company’s Texas utility, which is the smaller of the two utilities in TXNM’s portfolio. Over the five-year period, the company is planning to devote $4.165 billion in capital spending to its Texas operation, which is 53.40% of the $7.8 billion total. Of its total spending, only $3.417 billion, or 43.81% of the total, is set to be deployed in New Mexico.

The growth in Texas may not make much sense at first glance, given that two-thirds of the company’s customers are actually in New Mexico. However, it does start to make sense when we consider that Texas has been one of the states benefiting from the boom in electricity consumption due to data center construction and expansion. U.S. News and World Report states that Texas has three factors going for it that have been causing data center planners to favor the state over other options:

  • Relatively cheap energy prices,
  • Ease of connecting new facilities to the electric grid,
  • A business-friendly tax and regulatory environment.

There are currently 247 data centers located in Texas, and just about anyone who has ever tried to lease a server, for some reason, will attest to Texas usually being one of the locations offered by many web hosting or colocation providers. There are several more data centers in various planning stages that have planned locations in Texas. As such, the Electric Reliability Council of Texas projects that the demand for electricity in the state of Texas will almost double by 2030, due mostly to data centers, crypto-mining facilities, and similar technology-related projects.

This has not been lost on TXNM Energy, which has already seen the consumption of electricity within its service territory increase dramatically in the past few years. Back in 2020, the company’s Texas electric utility experienced a peak consumption of 1,804 megawatts. In 2024, the company’s peak consumption was 2,993 megawatts. That works out to a 13% compound annual growth rate over the five-year period:

Data centers use substantially more power than residential properties or most businesses. As such, while the company might be benefiting from positive demographics in New Mexico, the rising electricity demand from data centers in Texas might be an even larger source of growth for it going forward. The Rule of 72 tells us that if the demand for electricity continues to grow at a 13% compound annual growth rate, then consumption will double about every five-and-a-half years. That is a far greater growth rate than would be the case simply from the projected population growth in New Mexico. As such, it makes sense for the company to be directing a higher proportion of its capital spending towards the Texas utility, although it will be experiencing growth from both of them.

The growth program, as proposed, can be expected to grow the company’s rate base at a 12.4% compound annual growth rate over the 2025 to 2029 period, which is very high for a utility company. Readers of any of my articles on other utility companies will immediately notice this.

Financial Considerations

TXNM Energy will need to finance its projected capital expenditures somehow, as it will not be able to raise $7.8 billion simply through its ordinary operations. After all, the company’s annual revenues were only $1.9712 billion for the full-year 2024 period:

(All figures in millions of U.S. dollars.)

Its operating cash flows are even less, coming in at only 508.2 million in December 2024:

(All figures in millions of U.S. dollars.)

Given these numbers, there is no conceivable way that the company could raise $7.8 billion over a five-year period without relying on external financing (such as issuance of stocks, bonds, and other debt securities). TXNM Energy has stated that it intends to do exactly that, and unfortunately for common shareholders, the company is expecting to finance at least some of its growth through the issuance of equity that partially dilutes the existing shareholders.

The company states that it will issue about $1.3 billion in new equity over the 2025 to 2029 period to finance its growth program. At the current share price of $51.02 each, that would require the issuance of 25,480,203 additional shares, or about a 27.49% increase over the current 92.7 million shares outstanding. That is a fairly substantial increase that is far above the historical normal level for this company. This chart shows the company’s total outstanding shares over the 2015 to 2024 period:

(All figures in millions of common shares.)

As shown here, the company had 79.7 million shares outstanding back at the end of 2015. Thus, it increased its share count by 16.31% over the past ten years. The company’s newly proposed plan would increase it by 27.49% over a shorter five-year period. Thus, it appears that the shareholders will end up being diluted at a more rapid pace than was the case over the past decade.

With that said, the actual dilution suffered by TXNM shareholders will likely not be as bad as just calculated. After all, if the company’s share price increases, then it will not need to issue as many shares to raise the desired $1.3 billion. However, we can still see that shareholders will likely have to prepare for some dilution here. This means that the company’s earnings per share will rise at a lower rate than its net income, which could be disappointing.

As already mentioned, the company’s rate base can be expected to grow at a 12.4% compound annual growth rate over the 2025 to 2029 period. However, due to the pending share dilution, its investors should only expect 7% to 9% earnings per share growth. This is still not bad for a utility company, but its net income can be expected to grow more rapidly.

In the previous article on TXNM Energy, we saw that the company generally had a relatively high amount of leverage on its balance sheet when compared to its peers. Here is how it stands today:

Company Name

Net Debt-to-Equity Ratio

TXNM Energy

2.24

NorthWestern Energy Group

1.08

Avista Corporation

1.20

Otter Tail Corporation

0.45

Black Hills Corporation

1.22

Admittedly, this is a different peer group than the one that we used in our analysis last time. However, this different peer group still gives us the same result, which is that TXNM has a fairly high level of leverage compared to other electric utilities.

When we consider this, it is actually not very surprising that the company would opt to partially finance its capital investment program via the issuance of equity. If it were to finance its capital spending entirely through debt, then it would weaken the company’s balance sheet even more than it already is. The high ratio here could already be concerning to more risk-averse investors, and we do not want it to increase anymore. Thus, the proposed financing strategy does make sense, even though the dilution is not ideal.

Valuation

According to Zacks Investment Research, TXNM Energy will grow its earnings per share at a 2.98% rate over the next three to five years. This seems incredibly low, although Zacks does believe that its growth will come in much higher in 2026:

As we can see, Zacks Investment Research is projecting 2.92% earnings per share growth in 2025 over 2024 levels. The research firm then projects that the company will achieve 10.28% earnings per share growth in 2026. The 2026 figure is certainly reasonable, but the three-to-five-year projection of only 2.98% annual growth seems remarkably low given the positive demand fundamentals in Texas and the projected population growth in New Mexico.

The Zacks three-to-five-year growth estimate also makes TXNM Energy look incredibly expensive when compared to its peers:

Company Name

PEG Ratio

TXNM Energy

5.96

NorthWestern Energy Group

2.53

Avista Corporation

2.53

Otter Tail Corporation

N/A

Black Hills Corporation

2.70

(All figures from Zacks Investment Research.)

With the exception of Otter Tail, which will likely experience an earnings decline due to weakness in its manufacturing arm, all three of the other utilities are trading with price-to-earnings growth ratios in the mid-2s. TXNM Energy appears significantly more expensive, possessing a price-to-earnings growth ratio of 5.96.

However, this assumes that the Zacks medium-term growth estimate is correct. If in fact, the company grows its earnings per share at an 8% rate over the next three to five years, as was calculated earlier, then the company has a price-to-earnings growth ratio of 2.22 at today’s price, which is slightly better than its peers.

Conclusion

In conclusion, TXNM Energy could have some very real potential as a way to tap into the growing prevalence of data centers in the United States. The company has a large presence in Texas, and especially in the Dallas-Fort Worth corridor, where a great many new data centers are being built. This should be sufficient to grow the demand for the company’s electricity at very close to a 13% compound annual growth rate over the next five years, and roughly double it by the end of the decade. The company is also experiencing positive demographics in New Mexico, as the population of its service territory is growing overall.

The only real concerns here are that TXNM Energy, Inc. will have to dilute its shareholders to raise money to fund the needed expansion of its capacity, and it is already very highly leveraged. Even with this dilution, though, the company still appears poised to deliver a 7% to 9% earnings per share growth over the next five years. When combined with its 3.26% current yield, investors could very easily be looking at an 11% to 12% total average annual return, which is not bad at all for a sleepy utility company.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CrowdStrike: Leading The AI Cyber Arms Race
data.description:
There has been much talk about the positive implications that AI agents will increasingly have on the workforce. In fact, a wide array of industries are already witnessing large productivity gains as a result of early-stage AI agents like OpenAI's Deep Research or Manis AI. What is less talked about, however, is the major security implications truly capable AI agents will have. Increasingly capable autonomous AI agents working 24/7 to breach security systems, install malware, perform phishing activities, and all other manner of malicious activities will be a nightmare for enterprises and organizations to deal with.

All the powerful capabilities that autonomous AI agents will be capable of will almost certainly also be refactored for malicious activities, especially in a world where leading AI is increasingly open-sourced. This will allow state-actors, underground organizations, or even individuals to deploy incredibly powerful agentic AI to wreak havoc. In such an environment, cybersecurity is likely to become more important than ever. CrowdStrike (NASDAQ:CRWD) is not only weathering the advent of AI-driven threats, but positioning itself as an essential infrastructure for organizational security in this new era.

Agentic AI Raises the Stakes of Cybersecurity

The rise of truly capable agentic AI could legitimately revolutionize cyberattacks. AI agents are already able to reason, plan, and use a plethora of tools autonomously at a rudimentary level. In the inevitable scenario that AI agents continue to improve in capabilities, these agents' ability to independently probe networks, identify security flaws, exploit vulnerabilities, and even adapt strategies on the fly without needing a human handler means the need for equally effective countermeasures will be paramount. While a traditional single-prompt generative AI tool might help hackers write malware or phishing lures, agentic AI promises a far more dangerous paradigm in which attacks are executed end-to-end by AI.

In the incredibly high likelihood that such autonomous attacks will materialize at increasing scale, they would upend the modern cybersecurity landscape. Cybersecurity teams will be battling an endless swarm of cyberattacks that would likely be able to find increasingly sophisticated and even novel exploits through reinforcement learning. In the same way that AlphaGo used reinforcement learning to rapidly surpass human capabilities in Go, malicious AI could use the same techniques to dramatically surpass human cybercriminals. In other words, not only will agentic AI work far more tirelessly, as they are only limited by GPU usage, but they will likely also be far better at their stated goals.

This looming reality dramatically raises the bar for what will be acceptable in an organization's cyber defense. In fact, organizations will likely have to deploy their own AI-enhanced security capable of responding and adapting to AI-driven incursions in real time. This trend suggests an ever-escalating demand for leading security providers. Every new headline of AI-driven security breaches will likely reinforce the need for top-tier cybersecurity platforms, which is likely to act as a huge tailwind for arguably the most well-known and capable cybersecurity firm today, CrowdStrike.

CrowdStrike's AI-Native Platform

CrowdStrike has been preparing for the modern AI landscape for years now. The company has explicitly mentioned integrating agentic AI into its platforms in order to counter the growing threat landscape. In fact, CrowdStrike's popular Falcon Platform was specifically architected as a cloud-native, AI-powered platform used to aggregate trillions of security events that are eventually used to train machine learning models. The company's recently introduced Charlotte AI further pushes the company's AI capabilities by autonomously handling certain tasks. For instance, its Charlotte AI Detection Triage is an agentic AI that does first pass incident analysis by triaging incoming security alerts. It has already achieved 98% agreement with CrowdStrike's own expert analyst. This example of taking the human out of the loop allows for faster incident response and relieves workloads.

CrowdStrike's Falcon platform is well-suited for the age of autonomous threats. Its use of predictive analytics, behavioral AI, and increasingly agentic AI creates a self-reinforcing advantage. As more customers use its AI-native platform, the platform acquires more data, which allows for the creation of smarter models. This in turn translates into better threat prevention and more customers as a result. The early-mover advantage in this space is real, as is already playing out in the real world. In fact, the Falcon platform has been validated in independent tests and has achieved a 100% detection and protection rate in SE Labs' stress tests for advanced threats. In an age where AI capabilities are advancing at an incredibly fast pace, CrowdStrike's focus on staying ahead of the curve gives it an advantage over slower-moving competitors like Symantec or McAfee. Even among relatively fast-moving peers like SentinelOne (S) and Palo Alto Networks (PANW), CrowdStrike stands out in its ability to integrate AI into its products at a lightning pace.

Robust Growth

The bullish outlook is not based on hype or hope, but rather strong technology, forward-looking technology, and perhaps most important, strong financials to fuel growth. Despite posting an impressive ARR of $4.24 billion in Q4 2024, the company is still adding new customers at a rapid rate, with a net new ARR coming in at $224 million. The company's revenue, which increased 23% YoY, could even experience accelerating growth as agentic AI capabilities really take off. After all, truly capable agents have recently started going online and are increasing in capabilities at a staggering rate. Perhaps most important is CrowdStrike's record free cash flow of $1.07 during the quarter, which is an indication of the company's platform efficiency and disciplined execution. This also gives the company more than enough to invest heavily into its AI platform.

What is most telling of the quality of CrowdStrike's products is its deeply entrenched customer base. In fact, the company boasts an impressive 97% gross retention rate, which is an impressive figure even in a sticky industry. After all, CrowdStrike offers state-of-the-art mission-critical infrastructure in an industry that is only growing in importance. In addition, it is hard for customers to switch platforms given how entrenched its products are and given the fact that customers cannot afford to spend too much time without a cybersecurity provider, making any migration incredibly hard. Not only are customers largely sticking with CrowdStrike, but they are also steadily expanding their spend. In fact, 67% of customers use five or more of CrowdStrike's cloud modules, which is up from ~50% just two years ago. This breadth of adoption, whether it be in cloud workload security, identity threat protection, or endpoint protection, demonstrates the success of CrowdStrike's "land-and-expand" strategy.

Major Challenges Remain

While CrowdStrike has done a great job of staying at the cutting-edge so far, the cybersecurity market is only growing more intensely competitive. CrowdStrike's success is partly to blame for this growing competitive environment, as it has motivated many rivals, from legacy antivirus vendors to cloud-native upstarts, to fight for market share in the AI-driven cybersecurity space. What's more, technology giants are embedding their own security into their ecosystems. Microsoft (MSFT), for instance, has been rolling out its own AI-driven security agents. CrowdStrike's increasingly ambitious breadth of offerings is a challenge in and of itself, as it risks spreading itself too thin while competing against specialized competitors. For instance, while identity protection is only one small aspect of CrowdStrike's business, there are many competitors solely dedicated to this niche.

Another major risk is the increasing complexity and sophistication of agentic AI itself. The increasing unpredictability of the new threat landscape means that even CrowdStrike's cutting-edge platforms may struggle to adapt initially. A highly adaptive AI malware outbreak or a misfiring defensive AI are entirely new categories. CrowdStrike's own use of agentic AI, like its autonomous triage, also introduces a need for oversight to avoid false positives or negatives that can be exploited. Basically, the industry is entering uncharted territory, and CrowdStrike is leading the charge. This has the negative downside of CrowdStrike essentially being the test dummy of the new AI-driven threat landscape, which will inevitably come with bad press.

Conclusion

Despite the challenges facing CrowdStrike, the overarching narrative for CrowdStrike still remains incredibly compelling. The very advances in agentic AI that are already starting to embolden cybercriminals to perform increasingly sophisticated attacks are also serving as a major catalyst for businesses to beef up their defense spending. CrowdStrike stands out as arguably the primary beneficiary of this secular trend. While CrowdStrike is arguably expensive at its current market capitalization of ~91 billion and forward P/E of 94, the company still has far more room to grow as a result of the coming agent explosion.

data.title: Parsing European Supermajors: Shell Reigns Supreme
data.description:
Investing and choosing the right plays in European Big Oil isn't always cut and dry. The supermajors all have vastly different business profiles with different gives and takes, but as the recent market rout has shown, when things hit the fan, selling is indiscriminate. Performance across the major names that I personally cover and have working models on – BP (BP), Shell plc (NYSE:SHEL) (OTCPK:RYDAF), Eni (E), Total (TTE), Equinor (EQNR) – look oddly similar over the past few months.

Setting up a long position in a longstanding player like one of these companies often seems like a safe way to get long energy. But which one? In my opinion, given what we know about how 2025 is set to play out, Shell is the way to get exposure.

Credit Rating, Balance Sheet

Simplistically, when things get bad—or let's just call it volatile—we want to be long equities that have that cushion to ride out the storm. Small caps make the most sense at the bottom, and while I also buy and hold these throughout market volatility, it's certainly a more nauseous proposition than being in stalwarts. So, it makes sense that the oil and gas supermajors should have a lot of appeal on that measure across the board in today's environment.

Credit ratings agencies are a quick way to check how good these companies are versus one another. I'm a Moody's advocate personally, and Shell and Equinor have the best ratings of the majors (Aa2). It's notable that the only company I'm mentioning here that is not rated investment grade is Eni, so credit quality is high across the board.

For investors that don't trust the ratings agencies, these credit ratings largely follow their gearing (net debt to capital) and other financial metrics. Equinor and Shell have gearing in the 12.0–18.0% range; BP is the highest at close to 40.0%. Equinor and Shell have the lowest levels of debt relative to their assets, so going back to the credit ratings, it shouldn't be surprising that they also have the better outlooks, since that has become the de facto major metric that agencies use in evaluating risk in this area of the market. Investors can also use debt to EBITDA, but in this case, the “T” in EBITDA can cause some pretty large swings in actual retained cash flow / interest coverage given how high taxes are in Europe on these companies. While I typically use debt to EBITDA when analyzing North American shale, I get how gearing has become the norm for these companies—although it has its own weaknesses.

Earnings Sensitivity

Comparing these companies and their earnings outlooks for 2025 at current prices is an essay on its own. They don't just produce hydrocarbons: they pipe it, they market it, they refine it, they own petrochemicals assets, they have renewables businesses. At a high level, Shell looks to have the least downside to its overall earnings profile. That's something I think will be borne out over the coming weeks / months as bank analysts mark down their estimates to account for the new 2025 outlook.

To understand why, at a high level, remember that Shell is now primarily a natural gas player—not crude. It made a strategic pivot away from producing liquids, cemented when it acquired BG Group several years ago; 2025 production is more than 85.0% targeted towards natural gas. Most of the other companies like Eni or Total have relatively even production splits on a barrel of energy (boe) basis, and that means that as global natural gas and liquefied natural gas (LNG) prices have held relatively firm as crude oil has fallen.

Oil production is the hardest-hit business right now, then refining. Midstream, marketing, and renewables businesses largely look stable year over year. This means that the more diverse the supermajor's the business, the less downside they look to have. While the renewables businesses the European majors own have gotten a lot of heat lately from both investors and the Boards of these companies as they pivot back to fossil fuels because of returns, they are stable. That doesn't mean they are great assets—but the expectations for earnings generated from them look largely similar to the prior year.

I do want to point out that things really are not that bad. I fully expect each and every one of these companies to generate positive free cash flow this year. $60.00 Brent, or even $50.00 Brent, isn't a recipe for negative earnings and burning cash. But the point is that the delta between past and present expectations just has not and will not budge as much for Shell as it will for other companies.

Maintaining Shareholder Returns

Low debt and better earnings are a recipe for one thing: money back to investor pockets in one form or another. Every European supermajor has a different policy on shareholder returns. In the case of Shell, that is 40.0–50.0% of cash flow from operations; BP is 30.0–40.0% of cash flow from operations, for example. While I know that many are fearful given the drop in commodities and the share prices, the base dividend for all of the company that I cover will be covered, stress tested down to $55.00 per barrel Brent. We would need to get well below that to see these firms consider dividend cuts, as nearly all of them would be willing to (moderately) increase debt and gearing in order to avoid messing with the base payout.

That said, buyback cadence can and will change. The flexibility to engage in buybacks at these levels—given current Board of Director policy—has a lot of implications. Number one, it means that the share price has some form of internal support beyond market buying. And, of course, it also means the company can buy shares when the business is below mid-cycle earnings rates. After all, if a company is only buying back stock when cash flows are good and times are good, and not when they are bad, is that a great use of capital?

Assuming Brent averages a bit higher than current prices, I don't think buyback activity at Shell will drop at all. That was already better than most of the peer group outside of Total and has been / will be the highest in nominal dollars; buybacks at Equinor and BP were already expected to come down versus 2024. Since earnings sensitivity is not as high as others, and the balance sheet is in the best shape, even at lower prices we really won't see much change. Meanwhile, at BP, we could see buybacks down by two thirds versus 2024 levels.

Takeaways

In a sector where volatility is the norm and macro risks abound, Shell stands out as the most resilient and strategically well-positioned among the European supermajors. Its strong balance sheet, high credit quality, natural gas-focused production mix, and disciplined capital returns strategy make it the best risk-adjusted way to stay long energy in 2025. While the broader group will weather this storm and continue to deliver positive free cash flow, Shell’s lower earnings sensitivity and buyback stability set it apart. In a market that punishes weakness indiscriminately, owning strength matters—Shell has it.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Charles River Faces Significant Regulatory And Macro Pressures
data.description:
Shares of Charles River Laboratories (NYSE:CRL) have been a very poor performer over the past year, losing about sixty percent of their value. The pharmaceutical sector has been limiting its R&D spending, which has weighed on demand for the research and testing services that CRL provides. Recent news from the FDA on animal testing has exacerbated the situation. I last covered shares in November 2023, upgrading Charles River to a “hold.” Instead, I should have remained negative, with the stock down another 45% since then. It is now worth asking whether shares are finally worth buying or if more downside remains. I see no rush to buy shares.

Animal testing announcement is a major negative

Frankly, this company is facing an onslaught of macro and regulatory headwinds, largely outside of its control, which are simply too large to offset via cost cuts. The company was already facing pressure from smaller pharmaceutical budgets when bombshell news came out of the FDA Thursday. The FDA will be phasing out animal testing requirements, given ethical considerations and the ability to use AI and computer models.

While Robert F. Kennedy Jr. is undertaking significant and at times controversial changes in US healthcare policy, this action was not sparked by him. Instead, this shift was driven by a 2022 modernization law. Animal testing has long been a source of ethical debate, and improvements in technology are now more credibly creating alternative opportunities to test a drug’s safety.

This is just a roadmap, and it will take years to fully implement. However, because it is originally driven by legislation, I do not view this as a policy a future administration will reverse, though the speed of the shift could change. This has a meaningful impact on Charles River’s business.

As it begins its corporate overview in its most recent 10-K, CRL has “built upon our original core competency of laboratory animal medicine.” While it has diversified over time, animal testing is the original core of the business. The risk primarily sits inside its RMS, “research model services unit.” With $204 million of revenue in the company’s fourth quarter, RMS is about 20% of the business. However, its operating margin of 22.8% is higher than the business, so the potential impact to net income is higher.

New competitive threats could emerge

Now, CRL has been investing to move its exposure away from animal testing, but ~$300 million of spending is still fairly small. This also opens the door to more existential questions. Successful animal testing requires very specific skills, to keep animals healthy, safe, free from contamination etc, which CRL has built a core competency in. AI/computer modelling drugs is a different core competency. A company like Oracle (ORCL) or OpenAI would never have competed with CRL in the past, but they could in the future.

Essentially, a new testing regime opens the door to a new cohort of potential competitors from the tech sector, many of whom have significantly more spending power. If technology takes on a bigger and bigger role in testing drugs, more of CRL’s core franchises could be threatened. Now, this will not happen overnight, and the decline in the animal business will occur over years not months, but CRL’s competitive “moat” is increasingly narrowing.

Core business faces increasing pressure as budgets tighten

This comes as the core business was already struggling. In Q4, revenue fell 1% to $1.0 billion, and for the full year, it fell 1.9%, or 2.8% organically. While it has tried to reduce costs, its adjusted operating margin of 19.9% was down vs 20.3% in 2023. For the full year, earnings fell by 3% to $10.32. More encouragingly, in Q4, they were up by 8% to $2.66 thanks to lower taxes and some benefits from its cost-saving effort, though this growth will likely prove temporary. The company also had to take a $215 million impairment in its biologics testing business.

The problem CRL faces is that large biopharma firms are restructuring their pipeline, which is constraining spending into early 2025. Many companies reset budgets lower in mid-2024, and that is now rippling through CRL’s business. The pipelines and budgets tend to move slowly, and so a sudden uptick is unlikely, especially given all of the macro uncertainty.

Management had seen relative strength in small and mid-sized biotech firms, which had seen better funding conditions. While this may have been true two months ago, given where financial conditions are today, I am not sure it is anymore. Interest rates are higher, equity valuations are lower, and economic uncertainty is significant. While pharma is relatively noncyclical, the ability to access capital markets and raise venture funding is likely to be more difficult in the next six months than the last six months, which could reverse any growth CRL had seen.

On top of this, DOGE has been focused on government research contracts, and RFK Jr. has been instituting cuts at HHS. Academic and government exposure is 10% of the company. While the company has assumed some weakness here, I see the risk being a sharper decline, and it is difficult to envision this sector generating much growth for the duration of the Trump Administration.

Guidance faces pressure, though a weak dollar will help in 2025

Basically, CRL was facing declining big pharma spending and a weak government research environment. Now, biotech spending is at risk, and its core animal business faces significant regulatory pressure. The company has undertaken a $225 million restructuring effort with $175 million to be realized this year, but further cost cuts and unit rationalizations are likely. As you can see, it was guiding to about a 4.5% decline in organic revenue. This guidance assumed 2025 demand would be similar to 2024 levels.

This now appears too optimistic, and I expect a 5-10% decline in organic growth, though currency is now likely to be a tailwind given the weakness of the US dollar, so overall revenue may still come in down about 7%. However, the animal testing will be a longer-term drag with only a minimal impact to 2025, given its backlog and a multiyear phaseout. Ultimately, I do believe this directly impairs the business by 15-25% while also likely increasing competitive pressures on the remaining business for an impact that could be closer to 30%.

Valuation is not cheap enough to buy shares

Now, CRL is cash generative with about $500 million of 2024 free cash flow. It carries $200 million of cash and $2.2 billion of debt. Net leverage is a reasonable 2.2x. I would note that 1/3 of debt is floating rate, so if the Fed has to respond to higher inflation, that will be a negative. CRL had been targeting $350 million of repurchases. These buybacks were intended to be H1-weighted, so unfortunately, much was likely done at a substantially higher share price.

Given some likely pressure from the biotech sector offset by the dollar, I still see CRL earnings about $8.75-$9.35 this year. For a research company with a solid balance sheet, I do think a ~15x multiple would be appropriate or about $135. However, this EPS in 2025 will still include animal testing, a unit that will decline sharply over the next 3-5 years. Haircutting valuation by 25% to account for the elimination of this unit and increased competition, I get a fair value of $100.

At $93 where they are right now, shares have 7-8% upside. That is not enough cushion for me to be a buyer given the significant pressures this business faces that I have outlined. For those who already own the stock, I do not see a need to sell here—the pain has occurred, valuation is now fair, and it could bounce a bit given the sharp move, creating a better sell point. Given how much uncertainty there is, I would want shares at $85 or lower to buy to create enough margin of safety given how much pressure CRL faces.

As such, I view “CRL” as a hold, but I would not rush in based on its current P/E, as earnings face long-term pressure, which means this stock could be more of a value trap than a value opportunity.

data.title: December Non-OPEC And World Oil Production Rose
data.description:
A guest post by Ovi

The focus of this post is an overview of World oil production, along with a more detailed review of the top 11 Non-OPEC oil producing countries. OPEC production is covered in a separate post.

Below are a number of Crude plus Condensate (C + C) production charts, usually shortened to “oil”, for oil producing countries. The charts are created from data provided by the EIA’s International Energy Statistics and are updated to December 2024. This is the latest and most detailed/complete World oil production information available. Information from other sources such as OPEC, the STEO and country specific sites such as Brazil, Norway, Mexico, Argentina and China is used to provide a short-term outlook.

World oil production increased by 383 kb/d in December to 82,386 kb/d, green graph. The largest increases came from Brazil, 109 kb/d and US, 95 kb/d. January’s World oil production is projected to decrease by 165 kb/d to 82,121 kb/d.

This chart also projects World C + C production out to December 2026. It uses the April 2025 STEO report along with the International Energy Statistics to make the projection.

For December 2026, production is projected to be 83,936 kb/d. The December 2026 oil production is lower than the November 2018 peak by 656 kb/d. December 2026 production has been revised down by 187 kb/d from the previous report. December 2026 crude has been downwardly revised by 289 kb/d.

From December 2023 to December 2026, World oil production is estimated to increase by 920 kb/d.

December’s World oil output without the US increased by 288 kb/d to 68,896 kb/d. January’s production is expected to increase by 79 kb/d to 68,975 kb/d.

The projection is forecasting that December 2026 crude output will be 70,433 kb/d. Note that the December 2026 output is 2,252 kb/d lower than the November 2018 peak of 72,685 kb/d.

World oil production W/O the U.S. from December 2024 to December 2026 is forecast to increase by a total of 1,537 kb/d.

A Different Perspective on World Oil Production

Peak production in the Big 3 occurred in April 2020 at a rate of 34,739 kb/d. The peak was associated with a large production increase from Saudi Arabia. Post covid, production peaked at 34,010 kb/d in September 2022. The production decline since then is primarily due to cutbacks by Saudi Arabia and Russia.

December’s Big 3 oil production increased by 55 kb/d to 32,415 kb/d. Production in December was 1,595 kb/d lower than the September 2022 post pandemic high of 34,010 kb/d.

Production in the Remaining countries had been slowly increasing since the September 2020 low of 42,970 kb/d to December 2023. Production has been rising for the last three months and output in December increased by 334 kb/d to 49,977 kb/d and is now 509 kb/d lower than December 2023.

Countries Expected to Grow Oil Production

This chart was first posted a few of months backs and shows the combined oil production from five Non-OPEC countries, Argentina, Brazil, Canada, Guyana and the U.S., whose oil production is expected to grow. These five countries are often cited by OPEC and the IEA for being capable of meeting the increasing World oil demand for next year. For these five countries, production from April 2020 to August 2024 rose at an average rate of 1,199 kb/d/year, as shown by the orange OLS line.

To show the impact of US growth over the past 5 years, U.S. production was removed from the five countries and that graph is shown in red. The production growth slope for the remaining four countries has been reduced by 687 kb/d/yr to 512 kb/d/yr.

December production has been added to the five growers chart, up 236 kb/d to 23,322 kb/d. For the Five growers W/O U.S., December production rose by 141 kb/d to 9,832 kb/d, up 25 kb/d from December 2023.

The OLS lines have not been updated and will not be updated going forward unless additional production data provides a strong indication that production is rising.

Production up to December 2023 may be a situation where the past is not a good indicator of the future. In this case, it may be more important to focus on what has happened to production after December 2023 rather than before.

Countries Ranked by Oil Production

Above are listed the World’s 13th largest oil producing countries. In December 2024, these 13 countries produced 78.6% of the World’s oil. On a MoM basis, production increased by 114 kb/d in these 13 countries while on a YOY basis, production dropped by 696 kb/d.

December Non-OPEC Oil Production Charts

December’s Non-OPEC oil production increased by 300 kb/d to 53,425 kb/d.

Using data from the April 2025 STEO, a projection for Non-OPEC oil output was made for the period January 2025 to December 2026. (Red graph). Output is expected to reach 54,845 kb/d in December 2026. December 2026 was revised down by 70 kb/d from the previous report.

From December 2024 to December 2026, oil production in Non-OPEC countries is expected to increase by 1,420 kb/d.

December’s Non-OPEC W/O US oil production rose by 205 kb/d to 39,935 kb/d. January’s production is projected to rise by 48 kb/d to 39,983 kb/d.

From December 2024 to December 2026, production in Non-OPEC countries W/O the U.S. is expected to increase by 1,407 kb/d. December 2026 production is projected to be 172 kb/d higher than December 2019, essentially no growth over seven years. December 2026 production was revised up 125 kb/d from the last report to 41,342 kb/d

Non-OPEC Oil Countries Ranked by Production

Listed above are the World’s 11 largest Non-OPEC producers. The original criteria for inclusion in the table was that all of the countries produced more than 1,000 kb/d. Oman has recently fallen below 1,000 kb/d.

December’s production increased by 206 kb/d to 44,631kb/d for these eleven Non-OPEC countries, while as a whole the Non-OPEC countries saw a yearly production loss of 840 kb/d to 53,425 kb/d.

In December 2024, these 11 countries produced 83.6% of all Non-OPEC oil.

Non-OPEC Country’s Oil Production Charts

Angola’s December production dropped by 60 kb/d to 1,065 kb/d. Since early 2022 Angola’s production appears to have settled into a plateau phase between 1,100 kb/d and 1,200 kb/d. However December brought a drop below that plateau.

According to the March OPEC MOMR: “Growth is set to be driven by the US, Brazil, Canada and Norway, with the main decline anticipated in Angola. Is the December drop the beginning of the forecast decline for Angola?

The EIA reported that Brazil’s December production increased by 109 kb/d to 3,419 kb/d.

Brazil’s National Petroleum Association (BNPA) reported that production increased in January and February to 3,485 kb/d. The pre-salt graph tracks Brazil’s trend in the crude oil graph. For February, pre-salt production increased by 55 kb/d to 2,740 kb/d.

The December to February increase could be related to the addition of the two new floating platforms. The December OPEC report states that two new floating production storage and offloading (FPSO) platforms came online in November. It also mentions operational issues and slow ramp-ups in several offshore platforms continue to be an issue.

The March OPEC report states “Petrobras initiated production from the FPSO Almirante Tamandaré (Buzios 7) in mid-February at the Buzios field, located in the pre-salt layer of the Santos Basin, as the sixth production system in this field.” It also states that scheduled startup of planned new platforms could be delayed, “Nevertheless, operational issues and unplanned disruptions could potentially delay some scheduled start-ups from the platforms.”

Canada’s production increased by 41 kb/d in December to 5,030 kb/d a new record high. The increase is primarily due to site restarts related to wild fires in the oil sands area around Fort McMurray, the completion of maintenance and enhancements to oil sands cokers and bringing new SAGD sites online.

Canada has escaped US tariffs on its crude oil, natural gas and refined products according to this Article. Regardless it is not clear how the $10/b drop in WTI will affect production going forward.

“His administration’s latest sweeping tariffs on global trading partners exclude crude oil, natural gas and refined products, and Canadian goods, including energy, that are compliant with the Canada-United States-Mexico Agreement (CUSMA) will continue to move free of tariffs across the border.”

While no tariffs have been levied, Canada has woken up to the risks associated with becoming too close/dependant to a very close trading neighbour. There is much talk of building new pipelines to tidewater.

An election is scheduled in Canada for April 28. Both major parties are indicating a friendlier approach to oil sands production than the previous government.

The EIA reported China’s December oil output increased by 19 kb/d to 4,216 kb/d.

The China National Bureau of Statistics reported average production for January and February. The average came in at 4,335 kb/d. January/February production added 119 kb/d to 4,335 kb/d, red markers. This is a new high for China’s oil production. However note the slowing growth trend associated with the last three peaks. Has China reached peak production?

According to the EIA, Kazakhstan’s oil output decreased by 20 kb/d in December to 1,755 kb/d.

Kazakhstan’s recent pre-salt crude oil production, as reported by Argus, has been added to the chart. In October pre-salt crude production dropped by 120 kb/d to a low 1,340 kb/d. Since then production has risen by 450 kb/d. February production came in at 1,790 kb/d due to a New Field coming online. Note this is Crude whereas the EIA numbers are C + C.

Kazakhstan’s OPEC crude production target is 1,468 kb/d. At 1,790 kb/d, Kazakhstan is 322 kb/d over their target. According to this Article, Kazakhstan does not expect any major reduction in crude output in April after massively exceeding its Opec+ target in March.

Since Kazakhstan’s reduction promises have not materialized, OPEC announced it would “accelerate plans to revive halted supplies next month, with an increase triple the size originally scheduled”, according to this Article. The May production increase will be 411 kb/d.

Combined with the Trump tariffs the increase dropped the price of WTI/Brent by close to $10/b over two days. OPEC is trying to send a message to comply to Kazakhstan.

According to the EIA, Mexico’s December output dropped by 58 kb/d to 1,733 kb/d.

In June 2024, Pemex issued a new and modified oil production report for Heavy, Light and Extra Light oil. It is shown in blue in the chart and it appears that Mexico is not reporting condensate production when compared to the EIA report.

In earlier reports, the EIA would add close to 55 kb/d of condensate to the Pemex report. However for October and November it was increased to 122 kb/d and 117 kb/d respectively. For December, the condensate addition has returned close to its original increase of 55 kb/d. The December addition was 60 kb/d.

For January and February production, 60 kb/d have been added to the Pemex production to estimate Mexico’s January and February C + C production, red markers. Note that Mexico’s production, according to Pemex, has continued to fall every month since May 2023, except for one month. However, February’s oil production is little changed from January.

The EIA reported Norway’s December production rose by 59 kb/d to 1,810 kb/d.

Separately, the Norway Petroleum Directorate (NPD) reported that January production decreased by 24 kb/d to 1,786 kb/d and February dropped to 1,741 kb/d, red markers.

The Norway Petroleum Directorship reported that February’s oil production was 1.3% above forecast.

Last month S&P Global reported that the Johan Sverdrup oil field is expected to start declining from late-2024/early-2025.

“Europe’s highest producing oil field, Johan Sverdrup, is expected to start declining in late-2024 or early-2025 — earlier than expected — amid increased signs of water production at some wells”.

Are the January and February drops the beginnings of Norway’s steady oil production decline?

Oman’s production had risen very consistently since the low of May 2020. However, production began to drop in November 2022. According to the EIA, December’s output rose by 2 kb/d to 987 kb/d.

The EIA had been reporting flat output of 1,322 kb/d for Qatar since early 2022. However, the EIA has revised down all of the previous production data. Qatar’s December output was reported again to be 1,322 kb/d.

The EIA reported Russia’s December C + C production rose by 20 kb/d to 9,805 kb/d and was down 455 kb/d from March 2024. It is also 117 kb/d higher than the Argus Russia proxy estimate of 9,688 kb/d for December.

Unfortunately, S&P Global and Argus are not providing oil production information for the OPEC + countries as of March 2025.

This US production chart is very similar to the one published last week in the US update post. However the STEO portions of the two graphs have been updated using the April 2025 STEO.

Production in December 2026 at 13,503 kb/d is 195 kb/d lower than reported in the previous post. For the onshore L48, oil production starts to fall after August 2025 and production is anticipated to be 11,218 kb/d, revised down from 11,440 kb/d in the previous post.

Normally, Argentina oil production is not reported because its production is lower than 1,000 kb/d. However production has been steadily rising over the last five years due to the startup of shale oil and gas production in the Vaca Muerta basin.

According to this Article, “Vaca Muerta production could hit the 1Mb/d mark by the end of the decade, up from around 420,000b/d at the end of 2024. YPF and Vista Energy account for around 70% of production.”

In December, the EIA reported Argentina’a production rose 7 kb/d to 758 kb/d.

The Argentinian Energy Secretariat reported oil production in February dropped to 744 kb/d.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Arch Capital Group: Solid Insurer At A Bargain Price
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Patrick Maguire, CFA as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Summary

Arch Capital Group (NASDAQ:ACGL) is a large-cap, diversified insurance firm that has consistently grown book value and subsequent market price over the past decade. The management team at ACGL is highly experienced, now led by its long-term chief underwriter. Factors currently suppressing ACGL’s market price are likely temporary impediments to long-term further growth and profitability. Comparing against its peer group and its own historical price and performance, I believe ACGL is significantly undervalued.

There are times, sometimes extended times, in the markets when companies that grow at a moderate but steady pace are overlooked. Add in a couple of temporary headwinds, and these otherwise solid companies may appear altogether downtrodden. That is where the historically strong insurer, Arch Capital Group, finds itself currently. Based on its valuation relative to itself and its industry peers, ACGL is a downright bargain in my opinion.

Company Background

ACGL is a Bermuda-based insurance company with operations in the US, Canada, UK, and Australia and a current market cap of $36 billion (large-cap) in the S&P 500 Index (SP500). Its three main business segments are insurance, reinsurance, and mortgage insurance, which delivered underwriting income for 2024 of $1.6 billion, $1.2 billion, and $1.1 billion, respectively. The company’s strength has been strong underwriting expertise leading to steady and significant annual growth, essentially since its founding in 1995. At the time of my writing this article, ACGL is trading at ~$88, down from a high of $113 per share.

P&C Insurance Industry

The economics of the property and casualty (P&C) insurance industry are fairly straightforward, with low barriers to entry and significant competition. “Hard markets” are periods characterized by high premiums and strong profits, which seduce new entrants into the market, leading to subsequent “soft markets” of lower profitability. Many enter, but only one (OK, sometimes a few) will lead. The critical ability for success in the insurance industry is the management and pricing of risk, which includes judicious underwriting standards, particularly in soft markets. As ACGL’s management discusses in their 2024 annual report, an emphasis on “disciplined underwriting over premium growth” is paramount.

Management Team

The executive management team at ACGL has been with the firm for the long haul. One probable drag on recent market prices is the relatively sudden retirement of CEO, Marc Grandisson. Since the standard process for leadership transitions commonly involves more advanced warning, investors were rightly surprised and a bit skittish following the CEO’s abrupt departure. That said, the company and Mr. Grandisson have noted that his retirement was for personal reasons. While I am no insider, I can find no intel to suggest otherwise. Given the forthright nature of ACGL’s management historically, I do not see a major reason for alarm in this regard. The new CEO is Nicolas Papadopoulo, ACGL’s long-term chief underwriter, to whom much of the credit for the firm’s performance over the past decade may be attributed. While the CEO skill set undoubtedly differs from that of chief underwriter, Mr. Papadopoulo should be afforded a bit of runway before we make judgments on his performance in the role. The breadth and depth of the ACGL executive management team is impressive. Most have been with the firm for over a decade and three members, presidents David Gansberg and Maamoun Rajeh as well as CEO Papadopoulo, have served various roles at the company for over 20 years. They are an experienced group, committed to ACGL for the long haul.

Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)

Within the insurance industry, two common KPIs are loss ratio and combined ratio. The loss ratio = losses incurred/ premiums earned, while the combined ratio = losses + expenses incurred/ premiums earned. For both metrics, lower is better. Firms with combined ratios >100% are operating at a loss. As shown in the graph below, ACGL has done a solid job in recent years as measured by both ratios. Slippage in 2024 versus 2023 was due predominantly to losses incurred from Hurricanes Helene and Milton.

ACGL notes book value (BV) per share, the non-GAAP measure After Tax Operating Income (ATOI) per share, and Return on Average Common Equity (ROAE) as KPIs in their financial reports. The graph below shows the trend for the first two KPIs from 2020 through 2024.

The company has averaged 13% per annum in BV growth; a very lumpy 13%, but impressive growth nevertheless. The noted 13% rise in BV per share growth from 2023 to 2024 would actually have been ~24% had it not been for the $5 per share special dividend which management delivered back to its shareholders. ACGL’s annual report indicates ROAE rising from 14.8% in 2022 to 21.6% in 2023, with a drop back to 18.9% in 2024. The most recent year suffered due to the aforementioned hurricanes and California wildfires. These numbers for ACGL are above the industry average.

Valuation and Peer Comparison

A simple definition of the value of a business is all of the cash that can be extracted over its entire life, discounted back to the present. Thus, the discounted cash flow (DCF) method is a common way to value many types of businesses. However, it is not ideal for insurance firm valuation since free cash flow is difficult to determine due to the impact of firms’ investment portfolios as well as general lack of capital expenditures.

While the price to earnings multiple (P/E) has multiple limitations, it is a good starting point for fundamental valuation in most industries, including insurance firms. ACGL is currently trading at a trailing twelve-month (TTM) P/E of 7.6x, while its own median 10-year historical P/E is 12x. In this regard, the firm’s relative P/E is 72%. Given the powerful impact of regression to the mean in markets and in life, I am a fan of the relative P/E as one strong measure of a firm’s current valuation compared to its own history. Of course, awareness of the overall market P/E utilizing metrics like Cyclically Adjusted Price to Earnings (CAPE) ratio as described by Yale’s Dr. Robert Shiller is informative. Most desirable companies in the current market are trading near or above their median historical P/Es, so a deep discount as measured by relative P/E is an uncommon find today. For a very rough estimate of ACGL’s total value, multiplying a simple average of trailing and forecasted EPS of ~$10 per share (with no growth assumption) x 376 million shares outstanding x median historical P/E of 12x (also midrange for peers as noted in table below), we obtain $45 billion, indicating a 25% upside to the company’s current market cap of $36 billion.

For value investors, the critical determination that must be made is whether recent events (see below) that are negatively impacting a firm’s market price are temporary or permanent. In the former case, regression to the mean will likely play a powerful role in closing the gap between a company’s depressed current market price and its true intrinsic value. This process often does not occur within a year, let alone a single quarter. However, if one’s valuation is reasonably accurate, then Mr. Market generally sees the light and lifts out of his depression within 2–3 years. Of note, this time arbitrage is an important advantage available to the patient individual investor competing in the market versus professional investors whose performance results are measured over much shorter time horizons. Since both brain and computing power are hardly in short supply on Wall Street, I will take any potential advantage that comes my way. Finding a firm like ACGL with a strong and steady growth history trading at a significant discount to its median trailing P/E may indicate a level of downside protection that is crucial to most value investors.

Within the P&C industry, how does ACGL compare? The table below shows a few key fundamentals for ACGL versus several large cap peers (Chubb (CB), Fidelity National Financial (FNF), Hartford (HIG), Travelers Companies (TRV), and W. R. Berkley (WRB)). Based on P/E, relative P/E TTM vs 10-year historical median, and P/B, ACGL is less expensive than its peers, despite delivering an ROE at the high end of the group range.

ACGL CB FNF HIG TRV WRB
MC ($B) on 4/8/25 31.8 110 15.9 31.3 53 24.4
P/E 10 yr Median 12 13.8 14.2 10.7 12.7 14.5
P/E TTM 7.6 12.1 12.4 10.6 10.9 14.7
Relative P/E TTM 63% 88% 87% 99% 86% 101%
P/E FWD 10.4 12.6 10.1 10.1 13.3 14.3
Relative P/E FWD 87% 91% 71% 94% 105% 99%
P/B TTM 1.6 1.7 2.1 2 1.9 2.9
P/B FWD 1.4 1.5 1.7 1.8 1.1 2.5
ROE (%) 23 15 17 20 19 22

Source: Seeking Alpha, FullRatio

Recent Events

There are likely three factors dampening ACGL’s current market price. The first is the abrupt CEO transition noted above, likely not a long-term significant detriment to the firm, in my honest opinion. Second, multiple major catastrophic events, including the aforementioned two hurricanes as well as the rampant wildfires in California resulted in significant payouts, as demonstrated in the 2024 KPIs. Third is ACGL’s recent acquisition of US MidCorp and Entertainment businesses from Allianz (OTCPK:ALIZF). While some acquisitions can be rapidly accretive to earnings, this one was not. Again, only time will tell whether this acquisition was a wise capital allocation decision.

Risks

Mine is a simple definition of financial risk: probability of long-term or permanent capital loss. The best defense against this type of risk for investors in businesses is to ensure that the purchase price paid includes a margin of safety below the company’s estimated true value, as classically described by Benjamin Graham in “The Intelligent Investor.” While less useful, the academic definition of risk, volatility or standard deviation, does have some utility. Like most insurance companies, ACGL historically has been less volatile than the overall market as measured by beta (currently 0.6). Nevertheless, idiosyncratic risk comes with any individual stock purchase including ACGL, though it can be minimized by diversifying within a portfolio that contains other assets with low correlation.

Risks with insurance companies include the potential that significant losses are incurred and/or premiums fade over one’s investment time horizon. Underwriting risks due to poor assessment and/or pricing of client risk is unique to the insurance industry. Given the long-term, competent management team in place at ACGL, now led by its former chief underwriter, I believe this specific risk and general operational risk to be average to below average compared to its peers. Interest rate fluctuations impact insurance firms as companies invest their “float” from premiums received into their own investment portfolios (generally heavily weighted to fixed income securities) while awaiting the need to pay out on future claims. With its reinsurance business, ACGL is also at particular risk for needing to cover catastrophic losses from events like major hurricanes and the California wildfires.

Conclusion

ACGL, a large cap, diversified insurance firm with a straightforward business model, has a strong track record of steady growth in value. Despite a recent abrupt leadership transition, the company is led by a strong management team with both experience and expertise. Recent events impeding its market price are likely transient. The firm’s relative valuations to both its historical self and its peers indicate that ACGL is trading at a significant discount to its true value. Therefore, I rate this stock a Strong Buy.

data.title: Celestica: What's Fueling This Silent Climber
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Celestica (NYSE:CLS) is quietly building a compelling growth story with structural tailwinds in high-demand segments like communications hardware and hyperscale networking. Yet, the market hasn’t caught up. As the company leans into a capital-light model, improving cash generation and operational precision, its valuation still reflects outdated assumptions. This disconnect is where the opportunity lives.

Riding the Hyperscaler Wave: How Celestica's CCS Engine Is Powering Relentless Revenue Growth

Celestica holds fundamental strengths in its top line growth and segment performance. The company had a Q4 CY 2024 top line of $2.546B, with a YoY increase of $405M (~19%). This growth aligns with the high end of the company’s guidance range. Thus, Celestica is doing precise forecasting and good operational execution on it.

For CY 2024, Celestica derived revenues of $9.65B with a 21% YoY increase, pointing to a robust upward trend in top line performance over multiple periods. The CCS segment is a primary driver of this top line growth, with Q4 CY 2024 top line reaching $1.74B with a 30% YoY increase. This segment accounted for 68% of total company revenue in the quarter.

Inside CCS, the communications end market experienced 64% YoY revenue growth, with Hardware Platform Solutions (HPS) revenue increasing 65% to $807M and accounting for 32% of company revenue overall. The revenue growth is driven by strong demand from Hyperscalers for 400G and 800G networking switches and reflects how Celestica is taking advantage of high-demand tech trends. In short, CCS grew at a rate exceeding the overall company average, thereby pulling the consolidated top line upward.

In contrast, the ATS segment had revenue of $806M in Q4 CY 2024, remaining flat YoY and in line with guidance expectations. This segment comprised 32% of consolidated revenue, a decline in relative contribution against CCS. Within ATS, capital equipment hit ~30% YoY growth, offset by lower industrial market revenues, indicating a mixed performance that stabilizes overall ATS revenue. The revenue CAGR from CY 2022 to CY 2024 was 15%, which reflects a multi-year growth across both segments. However, CCS has outpaced ATS in recent quarters. The projected revenue for Q1 CY 2025 ranges from $2.475B to $2.625B, with a midpoint growth of 15%. Meanwhile, the CY 2025 outlook of $10.7B (11% growth) further supports a growth trend based mostly on CCS.

In short, Celestica’s strength is in leveraging the CCS segment’s high-growth areas in communications and HPS to offset flatter ATS performance. The 30% CCS growth rate against the flat ATS growth in Q4 CY 2024 marked a strategic focus on high-margin, high-demand markets, boosting overall revenue stability and forward growth. The 21% full-year revenue increase, coupled with a 15% CAGR over three years, led to a compounding effect that can support top line expansion as CCS may derive mid-double-digit growth in CY 2025. The consensus revenue estimates are projecting quarterly growth rates from 9.11% in Q2 CY 2025 to 18.14% in Q4 CY 2026, with CCS possibly driving these figures.

Interestingly, the relationship between FCF growth (11.6% Q4, 14.4% projected CY 2025) and top line growth (19% Q4, 11% CY 2025) suggests a high cash conversion rate. Here, FCF growth outpaces CapEx increases (1.5% to 1.9% of revenue). The $344M inventory reduction alongside $405M Q4 top line growth indicates an optimized supply chain that is reducing cash tied up in operations.

The 1x leverage ratio, down from 1.1x, combined with $1.2B liquidity, provides a buffer for strategic investments like the $75M to $100M research spend planned for CY 2025. The 14.4% FCF growth projection for CY 2025, also exceeding the 11% top line growth, suggests improving cash margins based on the 6.9% operating margin outlook. Therefore, the low CapEx-to-revenue ratio (1.7% in CY 2024, 1.5%-2% in CY 2025) indicates capital-light growth. So it allows Celestica to scale without proportional asset increases.

Overall, the inventory and deposit reductions with revenue increases reflect a lean operational model that supports scalability. This cash flow strength pushes Celestica to invest in high-growth areas like 800G and 1.6T networking programs, boosting edge and long-term top line growth prospects.

CLS Drops, But Valuation Signals Underrated Potential

What makes this attractive is not merely a forward P/E of 17x or a PEG under 0.6x, it’s the general trend: CLS consistently trades at deep discounts on revenue- and cash-flow-sensitive metrics that become more important when the market is tightening up. Forward EV/Sales stand at 0.9x, and trailing EV/EBITDA at 13x are not merely below industry standards; they’re well below them by 62% and 18%, respectively. They’re not superficial discounts. They are an indication that the market is valuing CLS on the assumption that its profitability will plateau when its operating leverage is increasing and its EBITDA multiple is 77% above its five-year average.

What’s more revealing is how CLS’s valuation has come unbuckled from its fundamentals. Both Price/Sales and EV/Sales are trading more than 60% below peers, but more than 100% above the company’s own five-year averages, indicating either an asset mispriced or an underlying market slow to catch up with the firm’s internal makeover.

Despite all this, the forward price-to-sales ratio is below 1.0 and below the short-term average of 0.83. If revenue climbs in upcoming quarters following the consensus estimates, the price of CLS stock can jump higher. For investors saturated in this market with priced-to-perfection plays and unwilling to bet against the narrative, this is the kind of disconnect that tends to cause stealthy but explosive re-ratings.

Revenue Dependence on CCS Threatens CY 2025 Growth if Segment Slows

Celestica exhibits a weakness in its revenue concentration and its heavy reliance on the CCS segment. In Q4 CY 2024, CCS holds the majority of the consolidated revenue. Meanwhile, the ATS segment represents 32% of all the revenue without YoY growth. This disparity points to possible stagnation of top line and reducing overall revenue diversification.

CCS’s outsized role means that any slowdown in the core markets, like Hyperscalers’ demand for 400G and 800G networking switches, could hit consolidated revenue. If hyperscalers reduce spending or shift suppliers, the 68% revenue share from CCS can reduce quickly. Conversely, ATS’s flat performance suggests limited capacity to offset a CCS downturn. In short, this segment imbalance creates a structural weakness.

Additionally, there is a margin divergence between segments that exposes another risk. If CCS volumes-mix shift unfavorably, overall margins can compress. CCS margin gains are coming from operating leverage and HPS revenue, but potential cost increases can offset these gains. The decline in the ATS margin points to sensitivity to industrial market softness, and lower volumes directly reduce leverage.

If this trend persists (ATS margins falling), the 6.8% company-wide operating margin can drop, as ATS’s 32% revenue share still impacts the aggregate. The projected 6.9% operating margin for CY 2025 is based on continued CCS strength, but a 1.1% CCS margin increase versus a 0.1% ATS decrease in Q4 CY 2024 suggests that total margin stability depends on CCS outpacing cost pressures. This is not a stable position amid inflation and supply chain disruptions from Trump’s tariffs.

Takeaway

Celestica offers investors a rare blend of accelerating growth, rising free cash flow, and deep valuation discounts, yet it’s flying under the radar. With dominant momentum in its CCS segment and capital-light scalability, CLS isn’t just undervalued, it’s mispriced. The setup favors a long-term upside before the market fully catches on.

data.title: Keros Therapeutics: Strategic Alternatives Could Bring About Value Creation
data.description:
Keros Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:KROS) suffered a major setback back in December 2024 when it announced that it had to halt its TROPOS phase 2 trial. This trial was evaluating the use of its drug cibotercept [KER-012] in combination with background therapy to treat patients with pulmonary arterial hypertension [PAH]. The reason for the halting of this mid-stage study was because of a safety review that was undertaken, whereby there were observations of pericardial effusion adverse events [AEs] in it.

This was definitely a huge blow to the company, and the hope is that it can somehow speak to the FDA, investigators, and regulatory authorities to attempt to move this program forward again. There is a risk that this program may not continue, but in light of this, there is something to consider in terms of a milestone that is rapidly approaching. It still expects to release topline data from this phase 2 TROPOS trial, using cibotercept for the treatment of patients with PAH, in Q2 of 2025. Besides this, there are actually two other reasons why it may still have some potential left. The first reason is that it still has an extensive pipeline.
